source: All-In Podcast — E274: SpaceX $2T case, Nvidia shock selloff
summary: Rapid reusability of Starship (multiple flights per day) is 1-2 years away and will create an insurmountable mass-to-orbit advantage for SpaceX
summary_extended: Baker argues that the critical distinction in the space launch industry is not reusability (30-60 day turnaround) but rapid reusability — flying the same rocket multiple times per day — and that Starship is specifically engineered to achieve this within approximately 1-2 years. He contends that while competitors like Blue Origin and China may eventually achieve basic reusability, rapid reusability creates a fundamentally different and insurmountable cost structure, driving mass-to-orbit costs asymptotically toward near-zero for SpaceX while competitors remain orders of magnitude more expensive. This cost advantage, Baker argues, is the enabling condition for moon and Mars colonization as well as orbital compute at scale — applications that require moving enormous mass cheaply and frequently. The investment implication centers entirely on SpaceX, which is privately held and not directly investable via US-listed equities; no publicly traded US equity is an unambiguous direct subject or beneficiary of this specific thesis without speculative inference.
detail: Baker distinguishes between reusability (30-60 day turnaround, where Blue Origin and China may eventually get) and rapid reusability (multiple flights per day), which is what Starship is designed for and what enables moon/Mars colonization and orbital compute at scale. He predicts rapid reusability in ~1-2 years. Even if competitors solve basic reusability, mass-to-orbit costs will asymptote to a very small number for everyone else.
confidence_evidence: Baker says 'I would guess a year or two, maybe sooner' — hedged but directional
quote_fragment: rapid reusability means you can fly the same rocket multiple times per day
